weeks.title: Week 1 — Backend testing: JUnit 5, Mockito, Spring slices
weeks.description: Make your Spring Boot code verifiably correct. Tests are what let you refactor fearlessly.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: JUnit 5 fundamentals

tasks.text: Learn: test lifecycle — @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, @BeforeAll, @AfterAll
tasks.description: Knowing the test lifecycle hooks lets you set up and tear down test data consistently across every test.

tasks.text: Learn: assertions — assertEquals, assertTrue, assertThrows, assertNotNull
tasks.description: These assertion methods are the core vocabulary of every test you'll write — get comfortable with them now.

tasks.text: Learn: @DisplayName for readable test names in the IDE
tasks.description: @DisplayName makes test failures readable at a glance, especially in CI logs with hundreds of tests.

tasks.text: Learn: @Nested — group related tests inside inner classes
tasks.description: @Nested groups related tests visually and lets you share setup within a context.

tasks.text: Learn: @ParameterizedTest with @ValueSource — run one test with multiple inputs
tasks.description: @ParameterizedTest avoids copy-pasting the same test for every input value.

tasks.text: Write: first tests for a pure utility class (e.g. price calculation logic)
tasks.description: Starting with a pure utility class avoids the complexity of mocking — a gentle on-ramp to testing.

days.resource: Rule: A good test has one reason to fail. If a test checks three things, split it into three tests. Short, focused, named clearly.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Mockito — isolate and test service logic

tasks.text: Learn: @Mock creates a fake — replaces real dependency (repository, email service)
tasks.description: @Mock lets you test a service in isolation without needing a real database.

tasks.text: Learn: @InjectMocks — creates the class under test, injects @Mock fields into it
tasks.description: @InjectMocks wires your mocks into the class under test automatically, saving manual constructor calls.

tasks.text: Learn: when(mock.method(arg)).thenReturn(value) — stub behaviour
tasks.description: Stubbing return values lets you control exactly what the dependency 'returns' for each test scenario.

tasks.text: Learn: verify(mock).method(arg) — assert a method was called with expected args
tasks.description: verify() confirms your service actually called the dependency it was supposed to — not just that it didn't crash.

tasks.text: Learn: assertThrows() — test that your service throws the right exception for bad input
tasks.description: assertThrows() is how you test your error-handling code paths, which are just as important as the happy path.

tasks.text: Write: ProductService tests — mock ProductRepository, test getById, create, delete
tasks.description: ProductService is core business logic — testing it with mocks is the foundation of your unit test suite.

tasks.text: Write: JwtService tests — pure Java, no mocks needed. Test generateToken(), extractUsername(), and isTokenValid() for both a fresh token and a token with a different username
tasks.description: Testing JwtService directly (no mocks) verifies the security-critical logic that the rest of your auth flow depends on.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Test coverage — what to test and what to skip

tasks.text: Learn: test business logic, not framework code — Spring's save() doesn't need testing
tasks.description: Focusing tests on your own logic — not Spring's internals — keeps your test suite fast and meaningful.

tasks.text: Learn: test every branch — if/else, null checks, exception paths
tasks.description: Untested branches are where bugs hide — covering every path catches edge cases before users do.

tasks.text: Learn: JaCoCo coverage report — add plugin to pom.xml, run mvn test, open target/site/jacoco
tasks.description: JaCoCo gives you a visual map of exactly which lines are untested, so you know where to focus.

tasks.text: Write: AuthService tests — test register (success, duplicate email), login (success, wrong password)
tasks.description: AuthService is security-critical — thorough tests here catch regressions before they become vulnerabilities.

tasks.text: Write: OrderService tests — test placeOrder (success, empty cart, product out of stock)
tasks.description: OrderService handles money and inventory — edge cases like empty carts or out-of-stock items must be tested.

tasks.text: Aim for 70%+ coverage on service layer — check JaCoCo report
tasks.description: 70% coverage on services is a realistic, professional target that balances thoroughness with effort.

days.resource: JaCoCo setup: Add jacoco-maven-plugin to pom.xml with prepare-agent and report goals. Run mvn verify, open target/site/jacoco/index.html in browser.

tasks.text: Learn: @WebMvcTest loads only the web layer — no DB, no full Spring context
tasks.description: @WebMvcTest is fast because it skips loading the database and full app context — ideal for testing controllers in isolation.

tasks.text: Learn: MockMvc — perform("GET /api/v1/products").andExpect(status().isOk())
tasks.description: MockMvc lets you simulate real HTTP requests and assert on the response without starting a server.

tasks.text: Learn: @MockBean — creates a Mockito mock and registers it as a Spring bean
tasks.description: @MockBean replaces a real dependency with a mock inside the Spring context — needed when the controller depends on a service.

tasks.text: Learn: jsonPath("$.content[0].name") — assert specific fields in JSON response
tasks.description: jsonPath lets you assert on specific fields deep inside a JSON response, not just the overall structure.

tasks.text: Write: ProductController tests — GET all (200), GET by invalid id (404), POST without auth (401)
tasks.description: Testing status codes for success, not-found, and unauthorized cases covers the most common API contract scenarios.

tasks.text: Write: test validation — POST with blank name returns 400 with field error message
tasks.description: Testing validation errors confirms your DTO annotations actually produce the 400 responses your frontend expects.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Integration tests with @SpringBootTest

tasks.text: Learn: @SpringBootTest loads full application context — slower but tests real wiring
tasks.description: @SpringBootTest catches wiring issues — like misconfigured beans — that mocked tests can't detect.

tasks.text: Learn: @AutoConfigureMockMvc — get a real MockMvc that goes through full security filter chain
tasks.description: @AutoConfigureMockMvc tests requests through your real security filters, including JWT validation.

tasks.text: Learn: use H2 in-memory DB for tests — add H2 dependency with test scope
tasks.description: H2 lets integration tests run against a real database without needing MySQL installed or running.

tasks.text: Configure: application-test.properties with H2 datasource, ddl-auto=create-drop
tasks.description: A separate test properties file keeps your test database isolated from development and production data.

tasks.text: Write: full auth integration test — POST /register → POST /login → assert JWT in response
tasks.description: An end-to-end auth test proves register and login work together exactly as a real user would experience them.

tasks.text: Write: product CRUD integration test — register, login, use token, create product, verify in DB
tasks.description: A full CRUD integration test verifies the entire request → security → service → database chain works correctly.

days.resource: Test pyramid: Many unit tests (fast, cheap) → fewer integration tests (slower, more realistic) → very few end-to-end tests. Don't invert this — it makes test suites painfully slow.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to write a Spring Boot integration test with @SpringBootTest, H2 in-memory database, and MockMvc — testing the full register → login → create product flow

weeks.title: Week 2 — Frontend testing: React Testing Library + Vitest
weeks.description: Test your React components the way users interact with them — not implementation details.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Setup Vitest + React Testing Library

tasks.text: Install: npm install -D vitest @testing-library/react @testing-library/user-event @testing-library/jest-dom jsdom
tasks.description: These packages are the standard testing stack for React + Vite — installing them sets up everything you'll use this week.

tasks.text: Configure vite.config.js — add test: { environment: 'jsdom', globals: true, setupFiles: './src/test/setup.js' }
tasks.description: Configuring Vitest in vite.config.js means your tests share the exact same build setup as your app.

tasks.text: Create src/test/setup.js — import '@testing-library/jest-dom' for custom matchers
tasks.description: jest-dom adds custom matchers like toBeInTheDocument() that make assertions far more readable.

tasks.text: Add test script to package.json: "test": "vitest", "test:ui": "vitest --ui"
tasks.description: Test scripts give you a quick way to run your suite from the terminal during development.

tasks.text: Write a trivial first test — render a Button, assert it's in the document
tasks.description: A trivial first test confirms your entire testing setup works before you write anything complex.

tasks.text: Run npm test — confirm it passes and the test runner is working
tasks.description: Running the test suite for the first time catches configuration issues early, before they block real test-writing.

days.resource: Why Vitest over Jest: Vitest is native to Vite — no extra config, shared vite.config.js, much faster. Same API as Jest so tutorials transfer directly.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Testing Library core concepts

tasks.text: Learn: render() — mounts a component into the test DOM
tasks.description: render() is the entry point for every component test — it's how you get a component into a testable DOM.

tasks.text: Learn: screen.getByRole, getByText, getByLabelText — prefer role queries (accessibility-first)
tasks.description: Role-based queries mirror how screen readers and real users find elements, making your tests more meaningful.

tasks.text: Learn: userEvent.click(), userEvent.type() — simulates real user input (better than fireEvent)
tasks.description: userEvent simulates real browser interactions more accurately than fireEvent, catching more realistic bugs.

tasks.text: Learn: expect(element).toBeInTheDocument(), toHaveTextContent(), toBeDisabled()
tasks.description: These matchers let you assert on visible UI state — exactly what a user would notice.

tasks.text: Write: ProductCard tests — renders name, price, image alt text correctly
tasks.description: ProductCard is used everywhere in your app — testing it ensures a core building block renders correctly.

tasks.text: Write: test clicking "Add to Cart" button calls the handler with correct product
tasks.description: Testing the click handler confirms the cart interaction actually works, not just that the button exists.

days.resource: Golden rule: Test what the user sees and does, not implementation details. Never test state variables directly — test the visible result of state changing.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Testing async components + mocking API calls

tasks.text: Learn: waitFor() — wait for async DOM updates after data loads
tasks.description: waitFor() is essential for testing components that fetch data — the DOM updates only after the async call resolves.

tasks.text: Learn: findByText() — like getByText but waits automatically (async-aware)
tasks.description: findByText combines waiting and querying in one step, simplifying async test code.

tasks.text: Learn: vi.mock('../api/products') — replace real API module with a mock
tasks.description: Mocking the API module isolates the component from network calls, making tests fast and deterministic.

tasks.text: Learn: wrap components in QueryClientProvider wrapper for TanStack Query tests
tasks.description: TanStack Query hooks require a QueryClientProvider — your test wrapper must provide one or hooks will throw.

tasks.text: Write: ProductsPage test — mock useProducts to return fake data, assert cards render
tasks.description: Testing ProductsPage with mocked data verifies the rendering logic without needing a real backend.

tasks.text: Write: test loading skeleton shows while isLoading is true
tasks.description: Testing the loading state ensures users get visual feedback while data is being fetched.

tasks.text: Write: test error message shows when query returns isError
tasks.description: Testing the error state ensures users see a helpful message instead of a blank or broken page.

tasks.text: Write: LoginPage test — type email and password, click submit, assert mutation called
tasks.description: Testing LoginPage end-to-end confirms the form correctly triggers the login mutation with the right data.

tasks.text: Write: test form validation — submit empty form, assert error messages appear
tasks.description: Form validation tests ensure users get immediate feedback on missing required fields.

tasks.text: Write: test password mismatch on RegisterPage shows correct error
tasks.description: Password mismatch is a common registration error — testing it ensures the UI guides users to fix it.

tasks.text: Write: CartContext test — add item, assert count increments, remove item, assert gone
tasks.description: CartContext is central state — testing it directly catches bugs before they manifest in the UI.

tasks.text: Write: test ProtectedRoute redirects to /login when user is not in AuthContext
tasks.description: Testing ProtectedRoute's redirect behavior confirms unauthenticated users can never reach guarded pages.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: MSW — mock the real API at network level

tasks.text: Install: npm install -D msw — Mock Service Worker intercepts fetch/Axios at the network layer
tasks.description: MSW intercepts requests at the network level, letting you test real Axios code paths including interceptors.

tasks.text: Create src/mocks/handlers.js — define handlers for GET /products, POST /auth/login etc.
tasks.description: Handlers define what your mock API returns for each endpoint — the foundation of MSW testing.

tasks.text: Setup MSW server in test/setup.js — beforeAll(server.listen), afterEach(server.resetHandlers), afterAll(server.close)
tasks.description: Wiring MSW into test setup ensures every test automatically has a working mock API, with handlers reset between tests.

tasks.text: Rewrite ProductsPage test to use real Axios instance — MSW intercepts and returns mock data
tasks.description: Switching to MSW tests your real Axios instance end-to-end, not just a mocked module.

tasks.text: Test error scenario: override handler for one test to return 500, assert error UI shows
tasks.description: Overriding a handler to return an error verifies your error-handling UI works under real failure conditions.

tasks.text: Write: full login flow integration test — MSW handlers for POST /auth/login and GET /auth/me, render App with MemoryRouter at /login, fill and submit the form, assert redirect to / and Navbar shows the user's name from /auth/me
tasks.description: A full login integration test is the strongest proof that auth, routing, and API integration all work together correctly.

days.resource: Why MSW: vi.mock() mocks the module — but MSW intercepts the actual HTTP request. It tests more of your real code (Axios config, interceptors, error handling) without a real server.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to set up MSW (Mock Service Worker) with Vitest and React Testing Library — including the handlers file and the test setup file, with one complete test example

weeks.title: Week 3 — Performance: backend and frontend
weeks.description: Find and fix real bottlenecks. Fast apps win users and clients.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Backend: the N+1 query problem

tasks.text: Learn: N+1 — fetching a list of 20 products triggers 21 queries (1 list + 1 per category join)
tasks.description: Understanding N+1 explains a subtle but extremely common performance bug in JPA applications.

tasks.text: Enable SQL logging: spring.jpa.show-sql=true, spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.format_sql=true
tasks.description: SQL logging is the diagnostic tool you need to actually see how many queries Hibernate generates.

tasks.text: Call GET /products — count the SQL queries in logs. More than 2? You have N+1.
tasks.description: Counting queries in the logs is how you confirm whether N+1 is actually happening in your app.

tasks.text: Fix with JOIN FETCH: @Query("SELECT p FROM Product p JOIN FETCH p.category")
tasks.description: JOIN FETCH tells Hibernate to load related entities in a single query instead of one per row.

tasks.text: Fix with @EntityGraph: @EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"category"}) on repository method
tasks.description: @EntityGraph is a declarative alternative to JOIN FETCH that keeps your repository methods clean.

tasks.text: Re-run the endpoint — confirm query count dropped to 1
tasks.description: Confirming the query count dropped proves your fix actually solved the performance problem.

days.resource: Warning: Disable show-sql in production — it floods logs and marginally impacts performance. Use it only during development investigation.

tasks.text: Learn: an index is a sorted lookup structure — makes WHERE and JOIN fast on large tables
tasks.description: Understanding indexes explains why some queries are instant and others get slower as tables grow.

tasks.text: Add indexes on columns used in WHERE/JOIN: category_id, created_at, name (if searched)
tasks.description: Indexing the columns your queries actually filter and join on is the highest-impact, lowest-effort optimization.

tasks.text: Learn: EXPLAIN SELECT ... in MySQL Workbench — reads the query execution plan
tasks.description: EXPLAIN shows you whether MySQL is using your indexes or doing a full table scan.

tasks.text: Insert 10,000 product rows with a SQL script — test query speed before and after indexing
tasks.description: Testing with realistic data volume reveals performance problems that are invisible with only a handful of rows.

tasks.text: Learn: never SELECT * in production queries — fetch only columns you need in projections
tasks.description: Avoiding SELECT * reduces data transfer and avoids accidentally exposing sensitive columns.

tasks.text: Use Spring Data JPA Projections — interface with only the fields needed for list views
tasks.description: Projections let you fetch only the fields a list view needs, reducing both query cost and payload size.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Backend: Spring caching with @Cacheable

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-cache
tasks.description: The caching starter provides the abstraction Spring needs to enable @Cacheable annotations.

tasks.text: Add @EnableCaching to your main class or a config class
tasks.description: @EnableCaching activates Spring's caching infrastructure — without it, @Cacheable does nothing.

tasks.text: Learn: @Cacheable("products") on service method — result cached on first call, served from memory after
tasks.description: @Cacheable avoids hitting the database for data that hasn't changed since the last request.

tasks.text: Learn: @CacheEvict("products") on create/update/delete — clears stale cache after mutation
tasks.description: @CacheEvict prevents stale data by clearing the cache whenever the underlying data changes.

tasks.text: Apply to: getAll categories (rarely changes), getProductById (frequently read)
tasks.description: Caching read-heavy, rarely-changing data like categories gives the biggest performance return for the least risk.

tasks.text: Test: call same endpoint twice, watch SQL logs — second call should produce zero DB queries
tasks.description: Watching SQL logs disappear on the second call is concrete proof the cache is working.

days.resource: Default cache: Spring uses a simple ConcurrentHashMap by default. For production with multiple instances, swap to Redis. For now the default is fine.

tasks.text: Install React DevTools browser extension — open the Profiler tab
tasks.description: The Profiler tab visualizes exactly which components render and why — essential for diagnosing slowness.

tasks.text: Record a session on the ProductsPage — identify components that re-render unnecessarily
tasks.description: Recording a real session shows you actual re-render patterns, not guesses.

tasks.text: Wrap pure child components in React.memo — prevents re-render when parent re-renders with same props
tasks.description: React.memo skips re-rendering a component when its props haven't changed.

tasks.text: Wrap callback props in useCallback — stable function reference prevents child re-renders
tasks.description: useCallback prevents a new function reference on every render, which would otherwise defeat React.memo.

tasks.text: Wrap expensive computations in useMemo — e.g. filtered/sorted product list derived from state
tasks.description: useMemo avoids recomputing expensive derived values (like filtered lists) on every render.

tasks.text: Re-run profiler — confirm the targeted components no longer re-render on unrelated state changes
tasks.description: Re-profiling confirms your optimizations actually reduced unnecessary renders, not just changed the code.

tasks.text: Install: npm install -D rollup-plugin-visualizer — analyse what's in your bundle
tasks.description: A bundle visualizer shows you exactly what's taking up space in your production build.

tasks.text: Run npm run build, open stats.html — find any surprisingly large dependencies
tasks.description: Reviewing the bundle reveals dependencies that are unexpectedly large or could be replaced/removed.

tasks.text: Confirm all pages are lazy-loaded — each route is a separate chunk in the output
tasks.description: Confirming route-based code splitting ensures users only download the code for the page they're viewing.

tasks.text: Convert large product images to WebP format — typically 30-50% smaller than JPEG
tasks.description: WebP images load faster with no visible quality loss, directly improving page load times.

tasks.text: Add loading="lazy" attribute to all below-the-fold img tags
tasks.description: Lazy-loading images defers downloading offscreen images until they're needed, speeding up initial load.

tasks.text: Run Lighthouse again — target 90+ Performance score on the live Vercel URL
tasks.description: A final Lighthouse run quantifies the cumulative impact of all your performance work this week.

weeks.askPrompt: Explain the N+1 query problem in Spring Boot JPA with a concrete example and show me three ways to fix it: JOIN FETCH, @EntityGraph, and a DTO projection query

weeks.title: Week 4 — Professional dev practices
weeks.description: Database migrations, API rate limiting, structured logging — things that separate junior from mid-level.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Flyway — database migrations

tasks.text: Add dependency: flyway-core — and remove ddl-auto=update from application.properties
tasks.description: Flyway gives you version-controlled, repeatable schema changes — the production-safe alternative to ddl-auto.

tasks.text: Create src/main/resources/db/migration/ folder
tasks.description: Migration files live in a dedicated folder so Flyway can find and apply them in order.

tasks.text: Learn: naming convention — V1__create_users_table.sql, V2__create_products_table.sql
tasks.description: Flyway's naming convention determines the order migrations run in — getting it right is essential.

tasks.text: Write V1 through V5 migration files matching your current schema from scratch
tasks.description: Recreating your schema from migrations proves they accurately capture your current database structure.

tasks.text: Drop and recreate your dev DB — let Flyway rebuild it from migrations, confirm it matches
tasks.description: Rebuilding the DB from scratch via Flyway is the real test that your migration history is complete and correct.

tasks.text: Write V6__add_product_slug_column.sql — add a new column to products, restart, verify applied
tasks.description: Adding a new migration for a schema change is exactly how you'll evolve the database going forward.

days.resource: Why this matters: ddl-auto=update is a dev convenience that silently breaks production. Flyway gives you versioned, repeatable, reviewable schema changes — the way every real team manages databases.

tasks.text: Audit all System.out.println() — replace every one with log.info() or log.debug()
tasks.description: println output is lost in production — proper logging is searchable, filterable, and level-aware.

tasks.text: Learn: log levels — ERROR (broken), WARN (suspicious), INFO (key events), DEBUG (verbose detail)
tasks.description: Log levels let you control verbosity per environment without changing code.

tasks.text: Learn: MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context) — attach request-scoped data (userId, requestId) to every log line
tasks.description: MDC lets you tag every log line from a request with shared context, making it possible to trace a single request across the whole system.

tasks.text: Build: a logging filter that generates a requestId per request, puts it in MDC
tasks.description: A request ID filter is what makes MDC actually useful — without it, there's nothing to attach to each request.

tasks.text: Configure logback-spring.xml to output JSON format logs for production (easier to query)
tasks.description: JSON-formatted logs are far easier to search and filter in production log aggregation tools.

tasks.text: Test: make a request, find the requestId in logs, trace the full request flow
tasks.description: Tracing a request by its ID end-to-end confirms your structured logging setup actually works.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: API rate limiting + request validation hardening

tasks.text: Add dependency: bucket4j-spring-boot-starter — token bucket rate limiting
tasks.description: Bucket4j provides a ready-made token bucket algorithm — no need to implement rate limiting from scratch.

tasks.text: Build: RateLimitFilter — limit auth endpoints to 10 requests per minute per IP
tasks.description: Rate limiting auth endpoints is a critical defense against brute-force login attempts.

tasks.text: Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header when limit exceeded
tasks.description: Returning 429 with Retry-After follows HTTP conventions clients understand for handling rate limits.

tasks.text: Review all DTO validations — add @Pattern for fields like phone, slug, URL
tasks.description: Pattern validation catches malformed input before it ever reaches your business logic or database.

tasks.text: Add @Size(max=255) on all String fields that map to VARCHAR columns — prevent DB truncation errors
tasks.description: Size limits on strings prevent database errors caused by data exceeding column length.

tasks.text: Test: hammer the login endpoint 15 times rapidly — confirm 429 after 10
tasks.description: Hammering the login endpoint is the only way to confirm your rate limiter actually triggers under load.

tasks.text: Add @Email to all email fields and @Positive to price/stock fields across DTOs — catches malformed emails and negative numbers before they hit the DB
tasks.description: Email and positive-number validation catch obviously invalid data at the API boundary, before it can corrupt your data.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Spring Boot Actuator + health monitoring

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-actuator
tasks.description: Actuator exposes built-in endpoints for monitoring your app's health and metrics.

tasks.text: Expose: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics
tasks.description: Explicitly listing exposed endpoints avoids accidentally exposing sensitive operational data.

tasks.text: Visit /actuator/health — confirms DB connection, disk space, and app status
tasks.description: /actuator/health is the endpoint deployment platforms use to verify your app is running correctly.

tasks.text: Visit /actuator/metrics — explore JVM memory, HTTP request counts, response times
tasks.description: Metrics give you visibility into memory usage and request performance without external tooling.

tasks.text: Secure actuator endpoints: only expose health publicly, require ADMIN role for others
tasks.description: Securing actuator endpoints prevents attackers from using them to gather information about your system.

tasks.text: Configure Railway to use /actuator/health as the health check URL for the service
tasks.description: Configuring Railway's health check ensures it can detect and restart a broken deployment automatically.

days.resource: Production tip: Railway and most PaaS platforms ping a health endpoint to decide if your app is ready to receive traffic. Actuator's /health is perfect for this.

tasks.text: Learn: main branch = production only. Never commit directly to main.
tasks.description: Treating main as production-only prevents half-finished work from accidentally going live.

tasks.text: Learn: develop branch = integration branch where features merge before going to main
tasks.description: A develop branch gives features a place to integrate and be tested together before reaching production.

tasks.text: Learn: feature/* branches — create one per feature, merge to develop via PR when done
tasks.description: Feature branches isolate work-in-progress, making code review and rollback easier.

tasks.text: Set up branch protection on GitHub: require PR review before merging to main
tasks.description: Branch protection enforces code review, catching mistakes before they reach main.

tasks.text: Practice: create feature/add-product-slug branch, add the slug feature, open a PR, merge it
tasks.description: Practicing the full feature-branch-to-PR workflow builds the muscle memory you'll use on every real team.

tasks.text: Learn: git rebase vs merge — use rebase to keep feature branch up to date cleanly
tasks.description: Understanding rebase vs merge helps you keep a clean, readable git history on your feature branches.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to set up Flyway database migrations in Spring Boot 3 — the Maven dependency, the migration file naming convention, and V1 through V3 migration SQL files for a users, products, and categories schema

weeks.title: Week 5 — Docker + GitHub Actions CI/CD
weeks.description: Containerise your app and automate testing and deployment on every push.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Docker fundamentals

tasks.text: Install Docker Desktop — confirm docker --version in terminal
tasks.description: Confirming Docker is installed is the prerequisite for everything else this week.

tasks.text: Learn: image vs container — image is the blueprint, container is the running instance
tasks.description: Understanding the image/container distinction is the foundational mental model for everything in Docker.

tasks.text: Learn: Dockerfile — FROM, WORKDIR, COPY, RUN, EXPOSE, CMD instructions
tasks.description: These Dockerfile instructions are the building blocks for packaging any application as a container.

tasks.text: Learn: docker build -t myapp . and docker run -p 8080:8080 myapp
tasks.description: Building and running your first image is the hands-on step that makes the concepts concrete.

tasks.text: Learn: docker ps, docker logs, docker stop, docker rm
tasks.description: These commands are your daily toolkit for managing running containers.

tasks.text: Pull and run: docker run -p 3306:3306 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=root mysql:8 — your own local MySQL in a container
tasks.description: Running MySQL in a container avoids installing it directly on your machine and matches how it'll run in production.

days.resource: Resource: docs.docker.com/get-started — the official tutorial is genuinely good and takes about 2 hours to complete.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Dockerise the Spring Boot backend

tasks.text: Build the JAR first: mvn clean package -DskipTests
tasks.description: Building the JAR first separates the Java build step from the Docker build, useful for debugging either independently.

tasks.text: Write a multi-stage Dockerfile: Stage 1 — Maven build. Stage 2 — slim JRE runtime only.
tasks.description: A multi-stage Dockerfile keeps your final image small by discarding build tools after compiling.

tasks.text: Use eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine as the runtime base — much smaller than full JDK
tasks.description: An Alpine JRE image is a fraction of the size of a full JDK image, reducing deploy time and attack surface.

tasks.text: Build and tag: docker build -t shop-backend:latest .
tasks.description: Tagging your image gives it a name you can reference when running or pushing it.

tasks.text: Run: docker run -p 8080:8080 --env-file .env shop-backend:latest
tasks.description: Running with --env-file mirrors how your app will receive configuration in production.

tasks.text: Test: hit localhost:8080/actuator/health — confirm app inside container is healthy
tasks.description: Hitting the health endpoint inside the container confirms the app actually started correctly in its containerized form.

tasks.text: Add a .dockerignore file — exclude target/, .env, .git, *.md so the build context stays small and fast
tasks.description: .dockerignore prevents unnecessary files from bloating the build context and slowing down builds.

days.resource: Multi-stage benefit: Build stage can be 500MB+ (Maven + JDK). Runtime stage with just JRE + JAR is typically 150–200MB. Smaller image = faster deploys, less attack surface.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Docker Compose — run the full stack locally

tasks.text: Learn: docker-compose.yml — defines multiple services (backend, db) as one unit
tasks.description: Compose lets you define your entire local stack — app and database — as a single reproducible configuration.

tasks.text: Write docker-compose.yml: services for mysql (image: mysql:8) and backend (build: .)
tasks.description: Defining both services in one file is what lets 'docker compose up' start your whole stack with one command.

tasks.text: Configure depends_on: backend waits for mysql to be healthy before starting
tasks.description: depends_on ensures the backend doesn't try to connect to a database that isn't ready yet.

tasks.text: Configure volumes: persist MySQL data so it survives container restarts
tasks.description: Volumes ensure your database data isn't wiped every time you restart the containers.

tasks.text: Run: docker compose up — both services start, Flyway runs migrations, app is ready
tasks.description: Running the full stack with one command is the payoff of all the Docker setup this week.

tasks.text: Run: docker compose down -v — stop and clean up. Rerun up — confirm clean start from scratch
tasks.description: Tearing down and rebuilding from scratch verifies your setup is fully reproducible, not dependent on leftover state.

tasks.text: Add a healthcheck to the mysql service (test: mysqladmin ping) and set backend's depends_on to condition: service_healthy — backend now waits until MySQL is actually ready
tasks.description: A proper healthcheck ensures the backend only starts after MySQL can actually accept connections, not just after the container process starts.

tasks.text: Learn: GitHub Actions — automated workflows triggered by git events (push, pull_request)
tasks.description: GitHub Actions automates checks on every push, catching problems before they're merged.

tasks.text: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml in the backend repo
tasks.description: The workflows folder is where GitHub looks for CI configuration — this is where automation begins.

tasks.text: Write: on push to any branch — run mvn test with Java 21 and an H2 test profile
tasks.description: Running tests automatically on every push catches regressions immediately, not days later.

tasks.text: Write: on push to main — also build Docker image and push to Docker Hub or GitHub Container Registry
tasks.description: Building and pushing a Docker image on merge to main automates the first half of your deployment pipeline.

tasks.text: Add GitHub Secrets: DOCKERHUB_USERNAME, DOCKERHUB_TOKEN (never hardcode credentials in yml)
tasks.description: GitHub Secrets keep credentials out of your repo while still letting workflows authenticate with external services.

tasks.text: Push a commit — watch the Actions tab in GitHub — green means all tests pass
tasks.description: Watching the Actions tab go green is the confirmation your CI pipeline actually works.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: GitHub Actions — frontend CI + full CD pipeline

tasks.text: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml in the React repo
tasks.description: A separate CI workflow for the frontend repo runs the checks specific to a React/Vite project.

tasks.text: Write: on pull request — run npm ci, npm test -- --run, npm run build
tasks.description: Running tests and a build on every PR catches frontend regressions before they're merged.

tasks.text: Write: on push to main — run tests, then trigger Vercel deploy via Vercel CLI or webhook
tasks.description: Triggering Vercel deploys from CI ties your deployment to passing tests, not just a push.

tasks.text: Backend CD: on push to main (after tests pass) — Railway auto-deploys from GitHub (already configured)
tasks.description: Railway's existing auto-deploy completes the backend half of your CD pipeline.

tasks.text: Test the full pipeline: push a small change → CI runs → tests green → both apps auto-deploy
tasks.description: Testing the full pipeline end-to-end confirms a single push can safely reach production for both apps.

tasks.text: Add a README badge: ![CI](https://github.com/yourname/repo/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)
tasks.description: A CI badge gives visitors instant visibility into your project's build health.

tasks.text: Add dependency caching to both CI workflows — actions/cache for ~/.m2 (backend) and ~/.npm (frontend) — compare build times before and after
tasks.description: Caching dependencies speeds up CI runs significantly, since Maven and npm don't need to redownload everything every time.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me a complete multi-stage Dockerfile for Spring Boot 3 with eclipse-temurin:21, and a docker-compose.yml that runs the backend and MySQL together with Flyway migrations

weeks.title: Phase 5 capstone — production-grade full-stack app
weeks.description: You've gone from zero to a professionally engineered, tested, containerised, CI/CD-deployed system.

projectCards.heading: What "production-grade" means now
projectCards.text: Your app is tested at unit, integration, and component level. The database schema is versioned with Flyway. Every secret lives in an environment variable. The codebase has no N+1 queries, rate limiting on auth endpoints, structured logs with request IDs, and a CI pipeline that runs tests on every push. Docker Compose lets any developer run the entire stack with one command.

tasks.text: All service classes have unit tests — JaCoCo shows 70%+ coverage
tasks.description: Solid unit test coverage is the foundation that lets you safely refactor and extend the app later.

tasks.text: At least one integration test covers the full auth + product flow
tasks.description: An integration test covering the full auth+product flow catches issues that unit tests in isolation can't.

tasks.text: React components have RTL tests — at least ProductCard, LoginPage, CartContext
tasks.description: Frontend tests on key components ensure the UI doesn't silently break as you make changes.

tasks.text: N+1 queries eliminated — confirmed by SQL log inspection
tasks.description: Eliminating N+1 queries ensures your app stays fast as data grows.

tasks.text: MySQL indexes on category_id, name (or any searchable column)
tasks.description: Indexes on commonly queried columns keep performance acceptable at scale.

tasks.text: @Cacheable on category list and individual product reads
tasks.description: Caching frequently-read, rarely-changed data reduces database load.

tasks.text: Flyway migrations replace ddl-auto — schema is fully version-controlled
tasks.description: Flyway migrations mean your schema changes are tracked, reviewable, and reproducible across environments.

tasks.text: Rate limiting on /auth/** endpoints — 429 returned after threshold
tasks.description: Rate limiting protects your auth endpoints from brute-force attacks.

tasks.text: Structured logging with requestId in every log line
tasks.description: Request IDs in logs let you trace any request through your entire system for debugging.

tasks.text: Actuator /health configured as Railway health check URL
tasks.description: A configured health check lets your hosting platform detect and recover from failures automatically.

tasks.text: Git branching: main/develop/feature/* — branch protection on main
tasks.description: A proper branching strategy with protection rules is how real teams collaborate without breaking production.

tasks.text: Docker Compose runs full stack locally in one command
tasks.description: One-command local setup makes it easy for anyone (including future you) to run the project.

tasks.text: GitHub Actions CI runs tests on every push — green badge on README
tasks.description: An automated CI badge signals to anyone viewing your repo that the project is actively tested and healthy.

tasks.text: Lighthouse 90+ on Performance and Accessibility on live Vercel URL
tasks.description: High Lighthouse scores demonstrate your app is fast and accessible — both matter to real users and employers.

tasks.text: Both repos have professional READMEs with architecture notes, setup guide, live URL
tasks.description: Professional READMEs are often the first thing a recruiter or collaborator sees — they need to make a strong impression.

tasks.text: Self-assessment: without notes, explain Flyway migrations, the multi-stage Docker build, how GitHub Actions CI runs your tests, and how you found and fixed an N+1 query
tasks.description: This final self-assessment confirms you can explain and defend every production-grade decision you made across Phase 5.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me a complete GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow file for a Spring Boot 3 backend — running JUnit tests on every push, and building and pushing a Docker image to Docker Hub on merge to main
